file_name: EMERALDHEALTHTHERAPEUTICSINC_06_10_2020-EX-4.5-CONSULTING AGREEMENT - DR. GAETANO MORELLO N.D. INC..txt

perturbation.original_text: (d) "Cause" includes, without limitation, the following: (i) the Contractor's commission of any act of gross negligence or gross incompetence in the conduct of his or her Services, or in the performance of his or her obligations under this Agreement; (ii) a material breach or default of any term of this Agreement by the Contractor if such material breach or default has not been remedied within 60 days after written notice of the material breach or default has been delivered by the Company to the Contractor; (iii) the Contractor dying or becoming permanently disabled or disabled (which includes, without limitation, mental infirmary or mental illness, drug or alcohol abuse or impairment, or any other physical or mental impairment that materially interferes with the individual's ability to perform his or her Duties) for a period exceeding 180 consecutive days or 180 days calculated on a cumulative basis over any two-year period during the term of this Agreement; or (iv) the Contractor's fraud, dishonesty or other material misconduct, wilful or otherwise, including, without limitation, the Contractor being: (A) convicted of a criminal offence involving fraud or dishonesty; or (B) sanctioned by a corporate registry, stock exchange, securities commission or other similar regulatory organization in respect of a material breach of corporate, commercial or securities rules, policies, laws or regulations. For the purposes of this definition and without limitation, Cause does not include a reduction in the Contractor's Fees or Perks implemented by the Company acting in good faith to respond to adverse market conditions, or in response to adverse cash flow issues then being faced by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) "Cause" includes, without limitation, the following: (i) the Contractor's commission of any act of gross negligence or gross incompetence in the conduct of his or her Services, or in the performance of his or her obligations under this Agreement; (ii) a material breach or default of any term of this Agreement by the Contractor if such material breach or default has not been remedied within 60 days after written notice of the material breach or default has been delivered by the Company to the Contractor; (iii) the Contractor dying or becoming permanently disabled or disabled for a period exceeding 180 consecutive days or 180 days calculated on a cumulative basis over any two-year period during the term of this Agreement; or (iv) the Contractor's fraud, dishonesty or other material misconduct, wilful or otherwise. For the purposes of this definition and without limitation, Cause does not include a reduction in the Contractor's Fees or Perks implemented by the Company acting in good faith to respond to adverse market conditions, or in response to adverse cash flow issues then being faced by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting clauses (A) and (B) from the definition of 'Cause,' specifically the instances where the Contractor might be convicted of a criminal offense or sanctioned by a regulatory body, an in-text contradiction is created. The contract still allows for termination for cause, but the specific examples related to legal and regulatory breaches are removed. This leads to uncertainty in enforcement because the termination rights are now more vague, especially when misconduct involves legal or regulatory issues. This omission contradicts Section 5.3 'Emeralds' Right to Terminate this Agreement for Cause' because, without these specific clauses, the scope of what constitutes 'Cause' is unclear when considering legal and regulatory non-compliance. This affects Emerald's ability to terminate the agreement decisively based on these breaches.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3 Emeralds' Right to Terminate this Agreement for Cause. The Company may terminate this Agreement and the Contractor's engagement for Cause at any time on written notice to the Contractor. The date of termination will be the date specified in the written notice and may be, in the sole discretion of the Company, the same day the notice is given to the Contractor, or such later date as the Company may decide.
perturbation.justification: The omission of clauses (A) and (B) from the definition of "Cause" weakens Emerald's ability to terminate the agreement for legal and regulatory breaches, creating a direct conflict with Section 5.3. The changed text narrows the definition of 'Cause' by removing instances where the Contractor might be convicted of a criminal offense or sanctioned by a regulatory body, which contradicts the original intent and enforceability of Section 5.3 'Emeralds' Right to Terminate this Agreement for Cause' because the scope of what constitutes 'Cause' is unclear when considering legal and regulatory non-compliance. Without those examples, the company's right to terminate under 5.3 is weakened.

perturbation.original_text: (g) "Constructive Termination" means the termination of the Contractor without Cause which shall mean: (i) a material adverse change in the Services of the Contractor, imposed unilaterally by the Company or the Board, such that the Contractor's level of seniority with the Company is materially diminished without Cause; (ii) a reduction in the then current Fee paid to the Contractor by the Company without Cause, which, continues for a period of time longer than 12 months; or (iii) a material reduction in the Perks received by, or the Fees which may be earned by, the Contractor from the Company without Cause, which continues for a period of greater than 12 months; other than a reduction in the Contractor's Fee or Perks implemented by the Company acting in good faith to respond to adverse market conditions, or in response to adverse cash flow issues then being faced by the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) "Constructive Termination" means the termination of the Contractor without Cause which shall mean: (i) a material adverse change in the Services of the Contractor, imposed unilaterally by the Company or the Board, such that the Contractor's level of seniority with the Company is materially diminished without Cause; (ii) a reduction in the then current Fee paid to the Contractor by the Company without Cause; or (iii) a material reduction in the Perks received by, or the Fees which may be earned by, the Contractor from the Company without Cause; other than a reduction in the Contractor's Fee or Perks implemented by the Company acting in good faith to respond to adverse market conditions, or in response to adverse cash flow issues then being faced by the Company.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clauses that specifies a continuous time period of 12 months for the reduction in fee/perks to be considered "Constructive Termination", this creates an in-text contradiction and makes the term ambiguous. The contract mentions in clause (ii) and (iii) that a reduction of fees and perks without Cause leads to constructive termination, but by removing the duration clauses, makes the term ambigious and less clear. This removal causes uncertainty because if the contractor leaves due to constructive termination, what happens. This contradicts with Section 5.5 Consequences of Termination of Agreement because the obligations after the constructive termination by the contractor, will be up to debate.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.5 Consequences of Termination of Agreement. All obligations of the Company to the Contractor hereunder shall immediately terminate and cease as of the date of the termination of the Contractor's engagement. The Company shall only be obliged to pay the Fees agreed to but not yet paid as of the date of termination, with such payment to be made within 30 days of the date of termination. The Company may terminate all access of the Contractor to the Company's premises and property as of that date.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 12-month duration requirement for a reduction in fees or perks to qualify as 'Constructive Termination.' This directly contradicts Section 5.5, which outlines the consequences of termination. Without the time constraint, any reduction in fees or perks, no matter how small or temporary, could potentially trigger constructive termination, leading to the obligations outlined in Section 5.5. This creates ambiguity and inconsistency, making the obligations after termination debatable and weakens the contract.

perturbation.original_text: 6.2 Confidentiality. "Confidential Information" means information disclosed to the Contractor as a consequence of or through its, his or her position as a director, officer, employee or consultant of Emerald, which information is not generally known in the industry in which Emerald operates. All Confidential Information will, during the Term of this Agreement and for a period of five years thereafter, be held by the Contractor in a fiduciary capacity for Emerald, in the strictest confidence, and will be used by the Contractor solely for the benefit of Emerald, and will not be used by the Contractor, directly or indirectly, for any purpose other than for the benefit of Emerald, nor will the Contractor divulge or communicate, directly or indirectly, such verbally, in writing or otherwise to any party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 Confidentiality. "Confidential Information" means information disclosed to the Contractor as a consequence of or through its, his or her position as a director, officer, employee or consultant of Emerald, which information is not generally known in the industry in which Emerald operates. All Confidential Information will, during the Term of this Agreement and thereafter, be held by the Contractor in a fiduciary capacity for Emerald, in the strictest confidence, and will be used by the Contractor solely for the benefit of Emerald, and will not be used by the Contractor, directly or indirectly, for any purpose other than for the benefit of Emerald, nor will the Contractor divulge or communicate, directly or indirectly, such verbally, in writing or otherwise to any party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the five-year limitation on the confidentiality obligation, the modified text now implies that the Contractor must maintain confidentiality indefinitely. This indefinite obligation creates an in-text contradiction because it conflicts with standard business practices where confidentiality agreements often have time limits. This now contradicts Section 5.5 Consequences of Termination of Agreement, because even after the agreement is terminated, the obligations for confidentiality, continues, indefinitely. This leads to uncertainty about whether and how this extended clause can be enforced, because it also, wasn't specify it can last beyond a certain period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.5 Consequences of Termination of Agreement. All obligations of the Company to the Contractor hereunder shall immediately terminate and cease as of the date of the termination of the Contractor's engagement. The Company shall only be obliged to pay the Fees agreed to but not yet paid as of the date of termination, with such payment to be made within 30 days of the date of termination. The Company may terminate all access of the Contractor to the Company's premises and property as of that date.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'five years thereafter' limitation on confidentiality, creating an indefinite obligation. This directly contradicts Section 5.5, which states that all obligations of the Company to the Contractor terminate upon the termination of the Contractor's engagement. By removing the time limit, the changed text implies the confidentiality obligation continues even after termination, contradicting the explicit termination of obligations stated in Section 5.5.
